metadata.paper_title: Towards a Reference Architecture for Cloud-based Plant Genotyping and Phenotyping Analysis Frameworks
metadata.venue: 2017 IEEE International Conference on Software Architecture (ICSA)

system_context.system_name: Plant Genotyping and Phenotyping Analysis Systems (Galaxy, iPlant, GenAp, LemnaTec)
system_context.system_description: Cloud-based and desktop frameworks designed to automate scientific workflows, manage large amounts of heterogeneous data (genomic and image-based), and support computational needs for plant trait appraisal.
system_context.business_goals: Ensuring global food security; facilitating reproducibility of experiments; high throughput processing of large/heterogeneous data; making analysis results available to researchers.

evaluation_setup.evaluation_goal: To investigate and analyze the architectures of candidate frameworks to determine their strengths and weaknesses, and to provide design recommendations for a reference architecture.

architectures.name: Galaxy
architectures.description: A web-based platform for biomedical research utilizing a Model-View-Controller pattern. It supports reusable software components, workflow management, and cloud infrastructure via CloudMan.

key_components.responsibilities: Responsible for adding and retrieving items from the relational database.
Executes jobs with allocation of resources and keeps track of jobs.
Collects and manages all types of tools in a language independent manner.
Enriched with libraries for various programming languages helping developers build applications.

architectures.name: iPlant Collaborative
architectures.description: A combination of independent applications and services supporting storage, computing, and analysis resources, utilizing a cloud service (Atmosphere) and a web interface (Discovery Environment).

key_components.responsibilities: Cloud service allowing users to launch virtual machines.
Primary web interface and platform to access computing, storage, and analysis application resources.

architectures.name: GenAp
architectures.description: An architecture integrated with a locally maintained Galaxy instance, featuring a data hub pipeline (MUGQIC) and individual server instance creation facilities.

key_components.responsibilities: Central database for storing smaller files and indexing data.

architectures.name: LemnaTec
architectures.description: A modular desktop application architecture for plant phenotyping, controlling hardware configuration, and recording/analyzing data.

scenarios.scenario_text: A biologist or clinician wants to explore their data using tools available in the web-based platform.
A computational biologist wants to use state-of-the-art pipelines to analyze their data without the burden of installing and maintaining all of the associated bioinformatics tools.
A sequencing centre pushes raw data into a Web based project created by a user to facilitate data access and analysis.
A computational agriculturist wants to annotate an image (leaf, root) with paint and brush (if possible), and also wants to save and update the annotation.
A developer wants to plug-in his image analysis tool or pipeline for doing image analysis, registration and segmentation.
A developer wants to upload an intermediate processed work flow data in the system for further analysis.
A computational biologist wants to add a new RNA analysis tool HISAT in the existing system.

A phenotypic researcher wants to work with a visual map interface to automatically extract environmental information (weather, soil info, landscape etc.) of a certain region and integrate it with plant traits analysis.
A user wants to share data from a project with another scientist to do joint data processing and exploration.
A computational agriculturist wants to convert a 8.6MB BAM file to a BED file for visualization.
Get an update after execution of each of the steps of a workflow or image processing pipeline.
A user wants to modify any part of code base of an available workflow and wants to save it in his private history as a new version of the workflow.

scenario_classification.rationale: Directly supported by Galaxy, iPlant, and GenAp as core functionality.
Supported by Galaxy and iPlant pipelines.
Candidates do not support raw data input directly; requires system modification.
Functionality unavailable in Galaxy and GenAp; requires new tool integration or web-based support.
Requires modification of configuration and tool modules in Galaxy.
Supported tool integration, though involves complex steps.
Systems lack support for weather/climate info; requires module updates.
Current systems execute in background without step-by-step updates; requires client/workflow design changes.
Feature not available; requires workflow architecture modification.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Add support for processing heterogeneous raw data or add a wrapper for Unified Data Interface.
issues_identified: Does not support processing of heterogeneous data.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Source code of Atmosphere, Models, and Services modules need to be changed; DE source code needs redesign.
Integrate a new tool in web version or add to Toolshed; modify Client, Scripts, and Lib.
issues_identified: Galaxy did not focus much on image processing pipelines or analysis.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Main Galaxy instance would need to be modified.
Update modules to support weather, climate, or soil information.
issues_identified: Does not support weather, climate or soil information for phenotyping analysis.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Input parameter list needs to be updated.
Code design needs to be updated to provide flash messages or notifications after each step.
issues_identified: User gets update only once the whole process executes or fails.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Models and Core functionality need to be updated.
Workflow architecture and code should be updated.
Models, Core, and Service design needs to change.

scenario_interactions.modularity_implication: System interchanges execution flow with numerous components, indicating it is a tightly coupled system.
Complex module interaction imposing heavy coupling; violations of separation of concerns principle regarding workflow management.

architectural_strengths: iPlant supports image processing and geo-spatial data.
Galaxy has a well-structured and intelligent workflow management system.
GenAp provides a distributed SQL interface for genomic data and supports MapReduce with pipelines.
architectural_weaknesses: Limited support for high throughput processing of large amounts of heterogeneous types of data.
Tightly coupled components in all architectures; lack of message queuing support.
Galaxy and GenAp do not provide image analysis pipelines for measuring plant leaf growth.
GenAp is slower due to HTTP communication with Galaxy.
iPlant components developed with different technologies make modification difficult.

architecture_comparison.rationale: Fastest performance for file conversion; flexible workflow creation; but tightly coupled and lacks built-in image processing pipelines.
Strong tool supporting most scenarios including image processing and geo-spatial data; however, less flexible workflow creation and performance degradation due to distributed subsystems.
Performance issues due to HTTP communication with Galaxy; fully dependent on third-party developers.
Desktop-based commercial tool; fails to provide support for collaborative research; limited web support.

recommendations: Adopt a reference architecture based on a data-centric model, software component model, and infrastructure model.
Use a unified data interface for interchanging input and processed data.
Implement a loosely coupled message-based structure for component interactions.
Define specific data-flow interfaces for each category of data.

inference_notes: Inferred rank based on the comparative analysis text descriptions (e.g., iPlant described as a 'strong tool', GenAp having performance issues).
